The sixth episode of the third season of "Blockchain Big Players," the first light variety show in the Web3 industry, jointly launched by Jinse Finance, Twinkle, and Dashu Finance, and produced by HTX DAO and OracleX, is officially scheduled to premiere on February 3, 2026.

Sixth Episode: Introduction to "Survivors"

If you have a natural "organizational buff," the sixth episode of "Survivors" is your stage! Five KOLs will form exclusive teams as captains, and 100 ordinary people will choose to join their factions to start intense team competition PKs. The winning team will share a cash prize.

data.title: OKX Brings Regulated Stablecoin Spending to Europe Through New Pay App and Mastercard Card
data.description:
Buying a morning coffee or paying for parking with digital assets has often felt more like a technical chore than a financial convenience.

OKX is changing that narrative by launching its new payment tools across Europe, aiming to turn stablecoins into a primary tool for daily life rather than just a trading asset.

By rolling out a specialized card and an integrated payment system, the exchange is attempting to bridge the gap between high-tech onchain markets and the local high street.

OKX Card is now live in Europe.

We’re modernizing money by bringing compliant onchain payments into everyday life.

Spend stablecoins seamlessly, with real-time conversion and no legacy payment friction.

This is stablecoin-first finance, built for the real world.

— OKX (@okx) January 28, 2026

Can Stablecoins Finally Replace Traditional Cash

While digital payment apps are already common in Europe, they often come with hidden costs.

Moving money across borders or waiting for transactions to settle can lead to fees between 1% and 5%.

OKX argues that the technology has finally caught up to the concept.

Stablecoins now offer near-instant settlement and operate 24/7, removing the friction and high costs of legacy systems.

The company views stablecoins as the bedrock of this transition, suggesting that these assets settle faster and cheaper than traditional bank rails while maintaining a steady value that avoids the volatility of older cryptocurrencies.

The New Money Era 🫡

— OKX (@okx) January 28, 2026

How Do OKX Pay and OKX Card Work Together

The system is built on two pillars designed to feel as familiar as a standard banking app.

Users can deposit euros into OKX Pay, convert them into fiat-backed stablecoins, and use those funds for everything from splitting dinner bills to paying monthly utilities.

For physical or online shopping, the OKX Card, a virtual debit product, connects directly to these balances.

It allows users to spend stablecoins wherever Mastercard is accepted, with the assets converting into euros at the exact moment of purchase.

OKX launches Europe's first compliant DeFi Pay and Card: stablecoin payments with instant settlement, lower fees and 24/7 Mastercard acceptance. EUR deposits convert to stablecoins at POS. Positive step for regulated DeFi access, but watch local compliance.

— Nifty (@nifty0x) January 28, 2026

This setup ensures that while the backend runs on blockchain infrastructure, the user experience remains simple and transparent.

Will Regulated DeFi Become a Daily Reality

The launch is not just about spending; it is about providing a safe way to access complex financial tools like Decentralised Finance (DeFi) and Real-World Assets (RWAs).

Within the European Union’s regulatory framework, OKX Pay acts as a gateway.

The same balance used for a grocery run can, where permitted, be used to interact with tokenised assets or onchain markets.

By operating through a regulated European entity and adhering to the Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) standards, the company aims to prove that innovation and oversight can coexist.

Why the Partnership with Monavate Matters

Security and compliance are central to the rollout.

The OKX Card is issued through Monavate, an Electronic Money Institution (EMI) that ensures all transactions meet strict Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Know Your Customer (KYC) standards.

This partnership is vital as the industry shifts toward heavy regulation.

Source: X

Interestingly, Monavate is currently in the process of being acquired by the self-custodial wallet provider Exodus as part of a deal announced in November 2025.

This acquisition highlights a broader trend of companies buying the infrastructure needed to control the entire payment process from wallet to merchant.

Is Crypto Spending Ready for the Mainstream

The timing of this launch coincides with a massive surge in digital asset usage.

Data from 2025 showed that net spending on Visa-linked crypto cards alone grew by 525%, jumping from $14.6 million in January to over $91.3 million by December.

🔥VISA CRYPTO CARD SPENDING JUMPS 525% IN 2025

Net spending across Visa’s major crypto cards surged 525% from $14.6 MILLION to $91.3 MILLION in 2025, led by strong usage of EtherFi cards. pic.twitter.com/fyDqlY1D1Y

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) January 5, 2026

OKX Europe CEO Erald Ghoos believes the card meets a fundamental human need to pay and be paid.

Ghoos said,

"[...] for the average user this can be technically challenging [...] With OKX Card, our users can realize this vision and transact freely and securely in real life […] while staying in total control of their assets."

By removing technical hurdles, the firm hopes to turn what was once an experimental technology into a routine financial habit.

data.title: Coinbase Moves to Let Businesses Mint Their Own Stablecoins
data.description:
Coinbase Edges Closer to Custom Stablecoins as USDF Enters Live Testing

Coinbase is taking another step toward turning stablecoins into a core business tool, quietly beginning backend testing of Flipcash’s upcoming USDF token as part of its new “Coinbase Custom Stablecoins” initiative. The move signals that branded, dollar-backed stablecoins for businesses may soon become a reality on one of the world’s largest crypto platforms.

Introducing Coinbase Custom Stablecoins.

Create a branded stablecoin for your business, backed 1:1 by collateral custodied by Coinbase.

→ Earn rewards on activity
→ Seamless interoperability across chains
→ Tap into Coinbase’s global distribution pic.twitter.com/DJ3Wcq4Zhn

— Coinbase 🛡️ (@coinbase) December 17, 2025

The exchange confirmed on Tuesday that USDF has been enabled internally on Coinbase Exchange for operational testing. While trading, deposits and withdrawals remain unavailable, the test phase marks the clearest sign yet that Coinbase is preparing to let companies issue their own customized stablecoins, collateralized by Circle’s USDC.

First introduced in December, Coinbase’s custom stablecoins feature is designed to allow businesses to issue branded, dollar-pegged tokens that can move seamlessly across Coinbase-supported blockchains. These tokens are expected to support a range of enterprise use cases, including payroll distribution, business-to-business payments, cross-border settlements and onchain treasury management.

Flipcash, a crypto infrastructure platform, is developing USDF, which is expected to launch publicly in early 2026 and serve as the primary stablecoin within Flipcash’s app ecosystem. Coinbase said the current test is strictly backend-focused, but the activation suggests the technical rails for third-party stablecoin issuance are already being put in place.

JUST IN ⚡️ Coinbase is advancing its custom stablecoin feature, now testing Flipcashs USDF.

The exchange is working with partners like Solflare and Flipcash to enable businesses to create their own branded, dollar-backed tokens. This feature, announced in December, will let… pic.twitter.com/t8P98W9hX8

— KOLYAN TREND (@kolyan_trend) January 28, 2026

Other crypto-native firms are also lining up. Solana-focused self-custody wallet Solflare and decentralized finance platform R2 are working with Coinbase to roll out their own customized stablecoin products, highlighting growing interest in application-specific digital dollars rather than one-size-fits-all tokens.

Stablecoins sit at the heart of Coinbase’s strategy

The push comes as stablecoins become an increasingly important pillar of Coinbase’s business model. The exchange generated nearly $247 million in stablecoin-related revenue in the fourth quarter alone, largely through its partnership with Circle, the issuer of USDC.

Total stablecoin market cap has hit a new all-time high, surpassing $310B and its previous high from December pic.twitter.com/eTissoHWGT

— Victus Global (@Victusglobal) January 22, 2026

As lawmakers debate new crypto market structure rules, Coinbase has been actively lobbying U.S. senators to ensure that stablecoin rewards and incentives are not restricted under future legislation. Any limits on yield or reward mechanisms could materially affect how custom stablecoins are adopted by businesses and users alike.

The broader market backdrop underscores why Coinbase is accelerating its efforts. The stablecoin sector currently holds a supply of more than $312 billion, and the U.S. Treasury has projected that figure could grow to $2 trillion by 2028. Bloomberg has gone even further, forecasting stablecoin payment flows to expand at a compound annual growth rate of 81%, reaching $56.6 trillion by 2030.

STANDARD CHARTERED WARNS STABLECOINS COULD DRAIN $500B FROM U.S. BANK DEPOSITS

Standard Chartered warned that stablecoins could pose up to a $500 billion risk to U.S. bank deposits by the end of 2028, highlighting potential disruption to traditional banking as digital dollars… pic.twitter.com/dfN0vXxHgz

— Crypto Town Hall (@Crypto_TownHall) January 28, 2026

As Coinbase quietly tests USDF behind the scenes, the message is becoming clearer: stablecoins are no longer just a trading pair or settlement tool. They are fast becoming programmable financial infrastructure, and Coinbase is positioning itself to sit at the center of that shift.

data.title: Sequoia's Disloyalty and the New God of AI: Silicon Valley Investment Discipline Dies in 2026
data.description:
The End of the Classical Era: Finix's Test of Loyalty>

In early 2020, the venture capital industry in Silicon Valley still followed a set of unwritten rules that had been in place for decades. In this system, a near-feudal relationship of loyalty formed between capital and startups: Once a top venture capital firm selects a "champion" in a particular sector, it pours resources, connections, and reputation into that partnership, forming a deep bond. In return, they promise not to fund the company's direct competitors—an ironclad discipline of loyalty and boundaries.

Sequoia Capital at that time was the perfect embodiment of this classical order. As Silicon Valley's most legendary venture capital firm, its authority came not only from its track record of turning lead into gold, but also from its mastery and adherence to these ethical rules.

In Sequoia's philosophy, clear boundaries are the foundation for building long-term trust, while ambiguous stances are the most dangerous poison in a network of relationships. It was against this backdrop that Sequoia faced a classic test. A startup called Finix came into Sequoia's view. Finix provided "payment infrastructure as a service," helping companies quickly embed payment processing functionality into their products. The problem was that this was precisely the core area of ​​payment giant Stripe—and Sequoia was one of Stripe's earliest and most important investors. According to the rules of the game in Silicon Valley at the time, investing in Finix would constitute a direct betrayal of Stripe. Media reports revealed Sequoia's final decision: to gracefully withdraw from negotiations, relinquishing potential board seats and financial rewards. This was not a simple trade relinquishment, but a public declaration of loyalty. At that time, Sequoia acted like a feudal lord, clearly aware of the boundaries of its territory and understanding that protecting existing relationships was far more important than seizing new opportunities. This restraint was not weakness, but a strength built on long-term strategic wisdom. The Breakdown of Rules: Sequoia's Three AI Trump Cards Four years is enough time to change dynasties in the tech world. When Sequoia sits at the negotiating table with OpenAI, Anthropic, and Elon Musk's xAI in 2026, the Sequoia that abandoned Finix in 2020 due to "potential competition" will seem like a distant memory. According to reports from the Financial Times and other authoritative media outlets, Sequoia Capital is deeply involved in a capital strategy that could reshape the global AI landscape: Building on its already significant investments in OpenAI and xAI, Sequoia is about to join a new round of investment in Anthropic. This round of financing targets a valuation of $350 billion—just four months ago, the company's valuation was "only" $170 billion. Even more noteworthy is the lineup of investors: Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC and US hedge fund Coatue each led the investment with $1.5 billion, while Microsoft and Nvidia pledged up to $15 billion, creating a grand picture of industrial and financial capital jointly betting. Sequoia Capital, a veteran Silicon Valley venture capital firm, no longer avoids conflict but systematically positions itself with all major AI players; it no longer chooses sides in the competition but attempts to become a holder of all sides. The once clear red line—"not to cooperate with direct competitors of existing portfolio companies"—has been completely erased and redrawn in the AI ​​era. This shift, seemingly sudden, is actually a rational choice in the face of the unique AI landscape. The core competency of traditional venture capital is "identifying patterns in uncertainty," but in the race of large models, the old evaluation framework has almost entirely failed. The AI ​​competition is a multi-dimensional, multi-stage, complex war: battles for underlying computing power, innovation in algorithm architecture, construction of data ecosystems, security and ethical dilemmas, and adaptation to regulatory policies… When uncertainty reaches this level, the optimal strategy shifts from “choosing the winner” to “covering all possible nodes.” Sequoia’s investment logic in the AI ​​field is no longer the traditional venture capital “horse racing,” but more like “buying a permanent seat in the casino”—it realizes that as intelligence becomes an infrastructure like electricity, what matters is not which company ultimately wins, but ensuring that it can stay at the table no matter what. Managing Conflict: Sequoia’s New Rules of the Game in AI Investment The phenomenal doubling of Anthropic’s valuation in four months reveals a profound shift in the capital market’s evaluation standards for AI companies. On the surface, the soaring valuation is directly related to revenue growth—the company's annualized revenue jumped from $1 billion to $10 billion. But the deeper driving force is an asset being repriced: systemic trust. Against the backdrop of rapid technological convergence, the core capability gap between various models is narrowing on a monthly basis. As the window of opportunity for technological advantage shortens, the decisive factor in competition shifts from a "performance race" in the laboratory to a "race to adopt" in the real world. The decisive factor is no longer "whose technology is stronger," but "who can be more safely, reliably, and compliantly adopted by governments, banks, hospitals, and multinational corporations." Anthropic's "Constitutional AI" framework—a method for enabling AI systems to align values ​​through human feedback and principle-based self-correction—while technically complex, sends a clear political signal: this is a company that prioritizes security, transparency, and controllability. This positioning precisely addresses the deepest concerns of corporate clients, government regulators, and the public. The massive investments by industry giants like Microsoft and Nvidia are essentially a vote for this "cooperability." They need more than just financial returns; they need a reliable, stable, and ethically sound partner to ensure the future of AI infrastructure. Sequoia's involvement adds financial capital backing to this trust, creating a complete chain of legitimacy. This means that the core competencies of top venture capitalists have been redefined. In the classical era, avoiding conflicts of interest was a professional virtue; in the AI ​​era, precisely managing multiple conflicts has become a rarer, more fundamental professional skill. What Sequoia is doing goes far beyond simply betting on a few more competitors—it is completing a paradigm shift in venture capital operations: from finding a single battlefield to learning to maintain a delicate balance across all fronts in a global war.

Building a new temple on the ruins of the old gods

Sequoia's turnaround is a rational transgression of capital. It has personally dismantled the classical order based on loyalty, and instead built a capitalist empire with itself as the hub.

Building a new temple on the ruins of the old gods

< This seemingly rational choice is giving rise to the most irrational future. When all top-tier capital adopts the same multilateral betting logic, the result will be a structural monopoly of innovation—a few institutions, through cross-shareholding, will effectively control the resource channels for all technological routes. Disruptive ideas not included in the capital map face a "resource death sentence" from their inception. A deeper transformation lies in the metamorphosis of the nature of power. When Sequoia Capital sits at the table alongside OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI, it has transformed from a "coach on the field" into a "league manager who controls all team tactics." The competition continues, but the rules have been redefined. Top-tier capital no longer predicts the future; instead, it meticulously lays the structural foundation for the future itself. While we marvel at sky-high valuations, we should be even more wary of the fact that the power to decide what deserves value is concentrating at an unprecedented rate at the center of the table.
